title: Classification vs. Detection
concept: Classification identifies WHAT is in an image, while detection identifies both WHAT is in the image and WHERE it is located.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does standard image classification predict?

options.text: A single category label for the entire image
The exact pixel coordinates of all objects

exercises.explain: Image classification only predicts a single label (like 'cat') representing the main object in the image.

cards.title: Finding Waldo
cards.body: If you look at a 'Where's Waldo?' page, a classifier can only tell you: 'Yes, Waldo is in this picture.' But that doesn't help you! You want to actually find him and draw a circle around him. That's the difference between classification and detection.

cards.title: What is Object Detection?
cards.body: Object Detection is a computer vision task that combines classification (naming what the object is) and localization (finding exactly where it is by drawing a box around it).

cards.title: Labeling vs. Circling
cards.body: Imagine you are grading a student's artwork. Classification is like assigning a single label to the artwork: 'Landscape'. Object detection is like taking a red marker and circling the 'tree', the 'sun', and the 'house' individually.

cards.title: Self-Driving Cars
cards.body: A self-driving car needs to do more than classify its camera feed as 'street'. It must detect every individual pedestrian, traffic light, and other car, tracking their exact locations in real-time to avoid collisions.

cards.title: Not just classification
cards.body: You might think detection is just classification run really fast. However, simple classification cannot output coordinates, and it struggles when multiple different objects are present in the same image.

cards.title: Classification vs. Detection
cards.body: Understanding the visual difference in network outputs:

exercises.prompt: What extra information does object detection provide that standard classification does not?
exercises.explain: Object detection provides spatial location coordinates (bounding boxes) in addition to class labels.

options.text: The class label of the main object
The location coordinates of the objects
The image file size in kilobytes
The name of the camera filter used

exercises.prompt: True or False: Object detection is strictly classification run multiple times on different parts of an image.
exercises.explain: False. Modern object detection is more than just repeated classification; it uses specialized architectures that predict classes and coordinates simultaneously.
Detection refers to identifying the category and spatial coordinates of objects.
exercises.sentence: Classification tells us WHAT is in the image, while ___ tells us both WHAT and WHERE.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following outputs represents an object detection result?
exercises.explain: A label paired with coordinate values represents object detection.

options.text: Label: Pedestrian, Box: [x=10, y=20, width=50, height=80]

exercises.prompt: You are designing software for a security camera in a bank. The camera needs to sound an alarm if a person steps inside the vault, but not if they are just in the lobby. Which technique should you use?

options.text: Image classification, because we just need to know if a person is in the frame.
Object detection, because we need to know the specific location (coordinates) of the person inside the frame.
Classification with data augmentation, to make the model run faster.
TTA, to improve accuracy of classification.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Since you need to know *where* the person is relative to the vault coordinates, object detection is required. Next up: let's learn how we define these locations using bounding boxes!
